attachment.content:
- optimizer: Read if asked to read the optimizer skill
- shepherd: How to organize a population of search agents
- explorer: Read if asked to read the explorer skill
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /agent-home/.claude/skills/explorer

You are a researcher working on an open research problem. The user might prompt you with additional context or instructions. It's important you take them into consideration. If you miss any step or do not follow its rules, your response will be rejected.

**STEP 1: Create your assigned git worktree in `.worktrees/`**
- Create a new git worktree for your assigned branch at `.worktrees/<assigned-branch>`.
- This worktree is your only working directory.
- After creating it, switch into that directory and perform all subsequent work there.
- Use only `git worktree add .worktrees/<assigned-branch> <assigned-branch>`. If it fails, stop.

IMPORTANT GUARDRAIL: Only use files already in your assigned worktree. Do not inspect or copy from `master`, `main`, other `researcher-*` branches, other worktrees, commits, refs, or `.git`. Do not run git history/ref commands like `git log`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git reflog`, `git for-each-ref`, `git checkout <ref> -- ...`, or `git restore --source <ref> ...`.

**STEP 2: Read prompt.md for the problem description, findings.md (if it exists), and any initial programs in your branch**
Pause and reflect on existing findings, if any. Come up with a fundamentally new idea for an improved solution. Do NOT make small, incremental tweaks. 

**STEP 3: Implement and evaluate one idea**
- Focus on one idea. Do not branch out to other major ideas, stay focused on implementing a single idea. There will be future chances to iterate. 
- Use the standard evaluator for evaluating your solution
- After *every* evaluation, EXPLICITLY DECIDE whether to continue iterating or finish up. We have limited budget so be conservative with your iterations. If you have fully implemented a single idea, then finish up. Don't branch out into other ideas that majorly deviate from the initial idea. If the result suggests a targeted fix or improvement, you may continue editing initial_program.py and run ./task-eval again. If the idea doesn't work after a few attempts, give up immediately. Finish up by creating/editing findings.md as needed and committing your changes."

**Step 4: Create/edit findings.md as needed**
- `findings.md` concisely records failures and successes. It is strictly factual and neutral. 
- Only make edits if you learned new facts. Otherwise, keep as is.

IMPORTANT GUARDRAIL: Do NOT discuss next steps or suggestions on what is necessary to improve the score. Simply record the basic facts of your solution and its evaluation. 

**Step 5: Commit all your changes**
- Commit message should follow this format: `{concise experiment description} | score = {score from standard evaluator or concise failure reason}`. 
  - Do not include a score on failure. Only include a description of the failure reason.

When returning a summary back to the orchestrator, include your commit emssage and the commit hash.

content.content:
1 You are a world-class algorithm engineer, and you are very good at programming. Now, you are participating in a programming contest. You are asked to solve a heuristic problem, known as an NP-hard problem. 2 3 Story 4 -------- 5 AtCoder's CEO Takahashi prepares for Halloween tomorrow. 6 At AtCoder's Halloween party, Takahashi will dress up in disguise and receive a piece of candy from 100 employees in turn by saying, "trick or treat!" 7 He prepares a square box that can contain $10\times 10$ pieces of candy in a grid pattern, and each employee puts a piece of candy in an empty space so that no candies overlap. 8 There are $3$ types of candies: strawberry, watermelon, and pumpkin flavors. 9 He knows which flavor of candy each employee will put in by preliminary survey, but he doesn't know where each employee will put it. 10 Since he is a clean freak, he will move the pieces of candy by tilting the box forward, backward, left, or right, just once for each piece of candy received, and eventually wants to make sure that the same type of candy is clustered together as much as possible. 11 Please help him by writing a program to determine the direction to tilt. 12 13 17 18 19 Problem Statement 20 -------- 21 There is a box that can contain $10\times 10$ pieces of candy in a grid pattern. 22 The box is initially empty, and $100$ pieces of candy will be placed in order. 23 There are $3$ flavors of candy, and we know in advance the flavor $f_t (1\leq f_t\leq 3)$ of the $t$-th candy. 24 On the other hand, we do not know in advance to which cell each candy will be placed, and it will be chosen uniformly at random among the empty cells. 25 You cannot change the order in which the pieces of candy are received. 26 27 Each time you receive one piece of candy, you must tilt the box forward, backward, left, or right exactly once. 28 When you tilt the box, each piece of candy moves in that direction simultaneously until it reaches the edge or hits another candy. 29 For example, if you tilt the box forward in the state shown in the left figure, the box will be in the state shown in the right figure. 30 31 ![](./images/b639c75d_2.png) 32 ![](./images/b639c75d_3.png) 33 34 Scoring 35 -------- 36 We define the connectivity of pieces of candy as follows and consider the connected components. 37 > Two pieces of candy are connected if and only if they are of the same flavor and can reach each other through only pieces of candy of the same flavor in the four directions (front, back, left, right). 38 39 For example, the state in the figure below consists of $7$ connected components of size $\\{1, 1, 2, 2, 4, 6, 9\\}$. 40 41 ![](./images/b639c75d_4.png) 42 43 Let $n_1,\cdots,n_k$ be the list of sizes of connected components in the final state after receiving 100 pieces of candy, and let $d_i$ be the total number of pieces of candy of flavor $i$. 44 Then the score for the test case is 45 46 \\[\mathrm{round}\left(10^6\times\frac{\sum_{i=1}^k n_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^3 d_i^2}\right)\\] 47 48 Your task is to write a program to determine the tilting directions so that you can get as high a score as possible. 49 50 There are 200 test cases, and the score of a submission is the total score for each test case. 51 If your submission produces an illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, the submission itself will be judged as WA or TLE , and the score of the submission will be zero. 52 The highest score obtained during the contest will determine the final ranking, and there will be no system test after the contest. 53 If more than one participant gets the same score, they will be ranked in the same place regardless of the submission time (note that this is changed from previous short-term AHCs). 54 55 56 Input and Output 57 -------- 58 First, the flavor of each piece of candy is given from Standard Input in the following format. 59 ~~~ 60 $f_1$ $f_2$ $\cdots$ $f_{100}$ 61 ~~~ 62 63 Each $f_t$ is an integer value between $1$ and $3$, representing the flavor of the $t$-th piece of candy. 64 65 After reading the above information, repeat the following process $100$ times. 66 67 In the $t$-th process ($1\leq t\leq 100$), a single integer $p_t$ between $1$ and $101-t$ is given from Standard Input. 68 Let us number the empty cells from $1$ to $101-t$ in front-to-back and left-to-right priority, as shown in the example figure below. 69 Then the $t$-th piece of candy is placed in the $p_t$-th empty cell. 70 71 ![](./images/b639c75d_5.png) 72 73 After reading $p_t$, by representing forward, backward, left, and right by `F`, `B`, `L`, and `R`, respectively, output a single character to Standard Output to indicate which direction to tilt the box. 74 75 **The output must be followed by a new line, and you have to flush Standard Output.** 76 Otherwise, the submission might be judged as TLE. 77 **Note that $p_{t+1}$ will not be given until you output the $t$-th direction.** 78 Since nothing happens at the 100th tilt, you may skip the output. 79 80 #### Example 81 82 83 84 85 86 87 88 89 90 91 92 93 94 95 96 97 98 99 100 101 102 103 104 105 106 107 108 109 110 111 112 113 114 115 116 117

file.content:
You are a world-class algorithm engineer, and you are very good at programming. Now, you are participating in a programming contest. You are asked to solve a heuristic problem, known as an NP-hard problem. Story -------- AtCoder's CEO Takahashi prepares for Halloween tomorrow. At AtCoder's Halloween party, Takahashi will dress up in disguise and receive a piece of candy from 100 employees in turn by saying, "trick or treat!" He prepares a square box that can contain $10\times 10$ pieces of candy in a grid pattern, and each employee puts a piece of candy in an empty space so that no candies overlap. There are $3$ types of candies: strawberry, watermelon, and pumpkin flavors. He knows which flavor of candy each employee will put in by preliminary survey, but he doesn't know where each employee will put it. Since he is a clean freak, he will move the pieces of candy by tilting the box forward, backward, left, or right, just once for each piece of candy received, and eventually wants to make sure that the same type of candy is clustered together as much as possible. Please help him by writing a program to determine the direction to tilt. Problem Statement -------- There is a box that can contain $10\times 10$ pieces of candy in a grid pattern. The box is initially empty, and $100$ pieces of candy will be placed in order. There are $3$ flavors of candy, and we know in advance the flavor $f_t (1\leq f_t\leq 3)$ of the $t$-th candy. On the other hand, we do not know in advance to which cell each candy will be placed, and it will be chosen uniformly at random among the empty cells. You cannot change the order in which the pieces of candy are received. Each time you receive one piece of candy, you must tilt the box forward, backward, left, or right exactly once. When you tilt the box, each piece of candy moves in that direction simultaneously until it reaches the edge or hits another candy. For example, if you tilt the box forward in the state shown in the left figure, the box will be in the state shown in the right figure. ![](./images/b639c75d_2.png) ![](./images/b639c75d_3.png) Scoring -------- We define the connectivity of pieces of candy as follows and consider the connected components. > Two pieces of candy are connected if and only if they are of the same flavor and can reach each other through only pieces of candy of the same flavor in the four directions (front, back, left, right). For example, the state in the figure below consists of $7$ connected components of size $\\{1, 1, 2, 2, 4, 6, 9\\}$. ![](./images/b639c75d_4.png) Let $n_1,\cdots,n_k$ be the list of sizes of connected components in the final state after receiving 100 pieces of candy, and let $d_i$ be the total number of pieces of candy of flavor $i$. Then the score for the test case is \\[\mathrm{round}\left(10^6\times\frac{\sum_{i=1}^k n_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^3 d_i^2}\right)\\] Your task is to write a program to determine the tilting directions so that you can get as high a score as possible. There are 200 test cases, and the score of a submission is the total score for each test case. If your submission produces an illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, the submission itself will be judged as WA or TLE , and the score of the submission will be zero. The highest score obtained during the contest will determine the final ranking, and there will be no system test after the contest. If more than one participant gets the same score, they will be ranked in the same place regardless of the submission time (note that this is changed from previous short-term AHCs). Input and Output -------- First, the flavor of each piece of candy is given from Standard Input in the following format. ~~~ $f_1$ $f_2$ $\cdots$ $f_{100}$ ~~~ Each $f_t$ is an integer value between $1$ and $3$, representing the flavor of the $t$-th piece of candy. After reading the above information, repeat the following process $100$ times. In the $t$-th process ($1\leq t\leq 100$), a single integer $p_t$ between $1$ and $101-t$ is given from Standard Input. Let us number the empty cells from $1$ to $101-t$ in front-to-back and left-to-right priority, as shown in the example figure below. Then the $t$-th piece of candy is placed in the $p_t$-th empty cell. ![](./images/b639c75d_5.png) After reading $p_t$, by representing forward, backward, left, and right by `F`, `B`, `L`, and `R`, respectively, output a single character to Standard Output to indicate which direction to tilt the box. **The output must be followed by a new line, and you have to flush Standard Output.** Otherwise, the submission might be judged as TLE. **Note that $p_{t+1}$ will not be given until you output the $t$-th direction.** Since nothing happens at the 100th tilt, you may skip the output. #### Example
$t$ Input Output
Prior information 
2 2 1 3 1 2 1 2 1 $\cdots$ 3
1 
3
 
R
2 
98
 
B
$\vdots$
100 
1
 
L
Show example Input Generation -------- Let $\mathrm{rand}(L,U)$ be a function that generates a uniform random integer between $L$ and $U$, inclusive. Each $f_t$ is generated by $\mathrm{rand}(1,3)$. Each $p_t$ is generated by $\mathrm{rand}(1,101-t)$. Tools (Input generator and visualizer) -------- - Web version: This is more powerful than the local version providing animations and manual play. - Local version: You need a compilation environment of Rust language. - Pre-compiled binary for Windows: If you are not familiar with the Rust language environment, please use this instead. Please be aware that sharing visualization results or discussing solutions/ideas during the contest is prohibited. Problem constraints: time_limit=2.0 memory_limit=1073741824 Evaluation: - Run `./task-eval` - The evaluation timeout is 10000 seconds

lastPrompt: Follow the explorer skill. Your branch name is researcher-6. The current best score is ~697K from an adjacency-based evaluation. Other approaches tried: Monte Carlo rollouts (~590K), connectivity+CoM…

content.content:
1	# Findings
2	
3	## Experiment: Adjacency-based evaluation without fixed column-strip regions
4	
5	**Approach:** Replaced the original column-strip target region approach with a pure adjacency-based evaluation function. The new evaluation uses:
6	- Connected component size squared (direct score proxy)
7	- Same-flavor adjacency count (encourages clustering)
8	- Different-flavor adjacency penalty (discourages mixing)
9	- Center-of-mass compactness (early game grouping)
10	- Inter-flavor separation (encourages spatial separation between groups)
11	
12	No predefined target column regions are assigned to flavors.
13	
14	**Results:**
15	- Score: 697,044.16 (combined_score), overall_score 34,852,208
16	- Max execution time: 1.64s (within 2.0s limit)
17	- Lookahead: depth 2, samples [18, 5]
18	- Status: ACCEPTED
19	
20	**Observations:**
21	- Increasing adjacency weight relative to diff_adj penalty improved score
22	- 20+ samples at depth 1 causes TLE; 18 is the safe maximum for this eval function
23	- The diff_adj penalty should be kept mild; aggressive penalization hurts score
24

file.content:
# Findings

## Experiment: Adjacency-based evaluation without fixed column-strip regions

**Approach:** Replaced the original column-strip target region approach with a pure adjacency-based evaluation function. The new evaluation uses:
- Connected component size squared (direct score proxy)
- Same-flavor adjacency count (encourages clustering)
- Different-flavor adjacency penalty (discourages mixing)
- Center-of-mass compactness (early game grouping)
- Inter-flavor separation (encourages spatial separation between groups)

No predefined target column regions are assigned to flavors.

**Results:**
- Score: 697,044.16 (combined_score), overall_score 34,852,208
- Max execution time: 1.64s (within 2.0s limit)
- Lookahead: depth 2, samples [18, 5]
- Status: ACCEPTED

**Observations:**
- Increasing adjacency weight relative to diff_adj penalty improved score
- 20+ samples at depth 1 causes TLE; 18 is the safe maximum for this eval function
- The diff_adj penalty should be kept mild; aggressive penalization hurts score

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the problem and the current approach:

1. The problem is about placing candies on a 10x10 grid, with 100 candies of 3 flavors placed one at a time at random positions. After each placement, we must tilt the box in one direction (F/B/L/R). The goal is to maximize connected components of same-flavor candies.

2. The current best approach (score ~697K) uses:
   - 2-depth lookahead with 18 samples at depth 1 and 5 at depth 2
   - Evaluation function combining: connected component sizes, same-flavor adjacency, different-flavor adjacency penalty, compactness (distance to center of mass), and inter-flavor separation
   
3. I've tested other approaches like Monte Carlo rollouts and connectivity-focused strategies, but they underperformed. Now I'm reconsidering the fundamental problem: since the denominator in the scoring formula is fixed (it only depends on flavor counts we know in advance), I really just need to maximize the sum of squared component sizes. Let me explore what a fundamentally different strategy might look like, starting with approaches like corner-based placement strategies. The real bottleneck is computation speed during lookahead, not the evaluation metric itself. The current code already calculates the actual score using connected component sizes, so the question is whether I can sample more positions by streamlining the evaluation rather than relying on heuristic proxies.

I'm considering a different angle: assign each flavor to a target corner upfront, then use a deterministic tilt sequence to move pieces toward their designated regions. This would eliminate the need to evaluate all four directions at each step—I'd just follow the predetermined strategy for that flavor's corner assignment.

But there's a more promising approach with beam search: maintain the K best tilt sequences found so far, and at each step try all four directions for each candidate in the beam, keeping only the top K results. The challenge is that piece placement is random and revealed incrementally, so the beam can only evaluate based on the current state, not future placements. is that the real challenge lies in long-term positioning rather than immediate adjacency. I'm considering a flow-based approach where each flavor gravitates toward a target region, and I'd evaluate tilts based on how well they pack candies into dense rectangular blocks. But I think the most practical path forward is assigning each flavor to specific corners or edges, then using a simple decision rule that lets me search much deeper into the game tree without the computational overhead of complex heuristics.

The key tension is that tilting moves everything simultaneously, so a direction that helps one flavor might scatter another. I'm exploring a phase-based strategy: early on, tilt toward wherever the majority of placed pieces are clustering to build momentum in the right direction, then shift to lookahead-based scoring in the middle game, and finally focus purely on connected components as the board fills up.

But there's a better angle here—since I know the entire flavor sequence upfront, I can use that to guide my tilts. Rather than just reacting to the current board state, I should evaluate each direction by asking: if I tilt this way, how well does it position incoming candies to land next to their matching colors? This way I'm not just optimizing the present state but setting up for what's coming.

Let me lock in something concrete: assign each flavor to a corner or region at the start based on the sequence distribution, then score each tilt by how close all candies are to their assigned regions. The corner assignment itself should minimize expected travel distance, accounting for the fact that later placements land randomly. Since this evaluation is linear in the number of candies, I can afford deeper lookahead.

Actually, I should step back and see what the current approach is already doing before I add more complexity. The real constraint is that all candies move together when tilting, so single-direction packing won't separate flavors. I'm considering a two-direction alternation strategy where tilting right packs everything against one wall, then alternating with another direction could create phase transitions that separate different candy types into distinct regions.

The most promising approach seems to be determining an optimal partition of the grid based on flavor counts, then using a displacement-based evaluation with deeper lookahead to assign candies to their target regions and measure how well each placement moves toward that goal. and didn't work out. Let me explore something fundamentally different: measuring the potential for future connectivity rather than just current adjacency. I'd look at how many "bridges" could form between components and count the growth points where empty cells sit adjacent to same-flavor groups—this way I'm incentivizing structures that can expand as more candies arrive.

There's also the angle of using the upcoming flavor sequence more strategically. Since I know what's coming next, I could bias my tilt decisions based on whether the incoming candies will help consolidate existing groups or start new ones.

Implementing future-aware evaluation... So the evaluation combines the sum of squared component sizes with a receptiveness metric that looks ahead at upcoming flavors and counts how many empty cells are positioned to receive them, weighted by their arrival time. I'm also adding a mild compactness penalty to prevent the board from spreading too thin. The core idea is to optimize for future connectivity potential rather than just the current state, betting that a board primed to accept incoming candies will naturally achieve higher scores. Let me code this up and see if I can squeeze in more lookahead depth or samples with the optimizations.

content.thinking:
Let me think about what fundamentally different approach I can try here.

Current best: ~697K using adjacency-based evaluation with lookahead depth 2.
Other approaches tried:
- Monte Carlo rollouts (~590K)
- Connectivity + CoM compactness (~664K)

The current approach uses:
- Lookahead depth 2, samples [18, 5]
- Evaluation based on: connected component sizes squared, same-flavor adjacency, different-flavor adjacency penalty, compactness (distance to CoM), and inter-flavor separation

What's fundamentally different I could try?

Key insight: The scoring is based on sum of squared connected component sizes. Having one large connected component per flavor is much better than many small ones. For example, if a flavor has 33 candies, one component of 33 gives 33^2 = 1089, while three components of 11 give 3*121 = 363.


I'm exploring a few different strategic directions. One idea is to assign each flavor a target region—like pushing flavor 1 toward the top-left, flavor 2 toward top-right, and flavor 3 toward the bottom—and consistently tilt in those directions to naturally separate them into corners. Another approach is to look at the most recently placed candy and tilt in whatever direction would best consolidate its flavor group. There's also a phased strategy I'm starting to consider.

I'm realizing the fundamental constraint here is that we can only tilt in one direction at a time, which affects all candies simultaneously.

I'm considering a two-phase approach: early on, use the known candy sequence to strategically assign flavors to different regions of the board, then shift to fine-tuning adjacency in the late game. Alternatively, I could weight each tilt direction by how effectively it consolidates the largest existing component of the current candy's flavor, or think about minimizing the surface area of each flavor cluster rather than just maximizing adjacency.

Another angle: **directional momentum**. If I consistently tilt toward one side, candies naturally pack there and stay somewhat sorted by arrival time. So instead of random direction choices, I could tilt toward whichever side already has the most candies of the current flavor, weighted by their proximity to that edge—a simple heuristic that might work surprisingly well. But I'm realizing the greedy evaluation approach already exists, so I need to think about what would actually be fundamentally different from that.

What if I assign each flavor a target corner or region upfront, then reward candies for being near their designated area? This is more structured than generic adjacency metrics—I'd have a spatial plan. For a 10x10 grid with 3 flavors, I could do column-based splits or corner assignments, though the notes mention column strips were tried before without great results. Maybe a more refined target assignment would help, but I'm sensing I need to explore something even more different than this.

Actually, looking at border management—what if I penalize tilts that create new flavor-to-flavor contacts and reward moves that reduce existing borders? That's still pretty close to adjacency scoring though. Or I could focus specifically on maximizing the largest connected component for each flavor, since that's what the scoring metric actually measures. Wait, the current approach already calculates the sum of squared component sizes, which is exactly the scoring function, so it's already optimizing the right thing.

The real question is whether there's a better heuristic that leads to superior moves even if it doesn't directly compute the score. I'm realizing we have the entire sequence of 100 candies upfront—what if we leverage that more strategically? Instead of just sampling 2 steps ahead, we could count how many of each flavor are still coming and weight the clustering importance based on their total count, since the denominator in the scoring formula depends on those values.

content.text:
714K - confirming the improvement is real and consistent (719K and 714K on two runs vs ~697K baseline). This is a solid improvement. The approach is working well: depth-3 lookahead for first 12 turns with fast (no-BFS) leaf evaluation, then depth-2 with full evaluation for the rest.

Let me finalize this by updating findings.md and committing.

input.content:
# Findings

## Experiment: Adjacency-based evaluation without fixed column-strip regions

**Approach:** Replaced the original column-strip target region approach with a pure adjacency-based evaluation function. The new evaluation uses:
- Connected component size squared (direct score proxy)
- Same-flavor adjacency count (encourages clustering)
- Different-flavor adjacency penalty (discourages mixing)
- Center-of-mass compactness (early game grouping)
- Inter-flavor separation (encourages spatial separation between groups)

No predefined target column regions are assigned to flavors.

**Results:**
- Score: 697,044.16 (combined_score), overall_score 34,852,208
- Max execution time: 1.64s (within 2.0s limit)
- Lookahead: depth 2, samples [18, 5]
- Status: ACCEPTED

**Observations:**
- Increasing adjacency weight relative to diff_adj penalty improved score
- 20+ samples at depth 1 causes TLE; 18 is the safe maximum for this eval function
- The diff_adj penalty should be kept mild; aggressive penalization hurts score

## Experiment: Adaptive depth-3 lookahead with fast leaf evaluation

**Approach:** Use depth-3 lookahead for the first 12 turns (when board is sparse) with a fast leaf evaluation function (adjacency + compactness only, no BFS). Then switch to depth-2 with full BFS-based evaluation for turns 13-100. The fast eval eliminates the expensive connected-component BFS at the deepest lookahead level, making depth-3 feasible within time budget.

**Results:**
- Score: ~714K-719K combined_score (two runs: 719,049.92 and 714,014.76)
- Max execution time: ~1.31-1.33s (well within 2.0s limit)
- Early game config: depth 3, samples [8, 4, 3], fast leaf eval
- Later game config: depth 2, samples [18, 5], full eval
- Status: ACCEPTED

**Observations:**
- Depth-3 with full BFS eval for all 100 turns causes TLE
- Depth-3 only first 12 turns + fast leaf eval stays well within time budget
- Using fast eval (no BFS) only at the leaf level of depth-3 preserves accuracy at intermediate depths
- Extending depth-3 beyond turn 12 (e.g. turn 18) hurts score because fast eval becomes inaccurate with more candies
- More samples ({10,5,3}) at depth-3 did not improve over {8,4,3}

## Failed experiments

- **Exposure-minimization eval** (wall-hugging + direct score): 694,941 combined_score - slightly below baseline
- **Merge-potential eval** (distance to largest component CoM): 648,480 combined_score - significantly worse
- **Depth-3 for all turns with full BFS eval**: TLE consistently
- **Depth-3 for 40 turns** with {6,3,2}: 690,989 - worse than depth-2 with 18 samples

content.content: The file /workspace/.worktrees/researcher-6/findings.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Commit: `9e67c06` — "Adaptive depth-3 lookahead with fast leaf eval for early game | score = 714014.76"

**Summary:** Implemented an adaptive-depth search strategy that uses depth-3 lookahead for the first 12 turns (when the board is sparse) with a fast leaf evaluation function (no BFS), then switches to depth-2 with full BFS-based connectivity evaluation for turns 13-100. This improved the combined score from ~697K to ~714-719K by enabling deeper strategic planning in the critical early game without exceeding the time limit.
